metadata.title: From Christ to Compassion: The Changing Language of Pastoral Care
metadata.journal: Journal for the Scientific Study of Religion

abstract: The rise of neurology, psychology, and psychiatry over the last 100 years has challenged the clergy's historical monopoly on dealing with "personal problems" and mental well-being. In this study, I document the changing language of pastoral care by analyzing over seventy years of academic articles in the Journal of Pastoral Care and Counseling (N = 4,054) using structural topic modeling. Ultimately, I reveal a linguistic shift from the universal to the particular as pastoral care professionals drop language of human nature and morality for that of individual narratives. I also find a decline of overtly religious language since the 1950s in favor of a more ecumenical language of spirituality, hope, and presence. Both of these trends take place alongside a push for "evidencebased" pastoral care. Together, these linguistic shifts offer insight into a seventy-year struggle to provide authentic religious care in a world of competing alternatives.

paragraphs: This study documents the declining authority of pastoral care professionals in the 20th century. With the rise of neurology in the late 19th century, psychology in the early 20th century, and psychiatry shortly thereafter, clergy have experienced a slow but relentless challenge to their jurisdictional authority with regard to problems of personal and mental well-being. Narrowing previous macro-discussions of secularization, I pair Chaves ' (1994) theory of declining religious authority with Abbott's (1988) theory of professional systems to document the shifting language used by pastoral care professionals. Ultimately, I demonstrate how pastoral care professionals today rely on an individualistic phenomenology of personal narrative and increasingly employ inclusive pluralist theologies while attempting to balance the push for specificity and evidencebased treatments. I do this combining a historical analysis of pastoral care in 20th-century America with a quantitative analysis of over seventy years of academic articles in the Journal of Pastoral Care and Counseling (JPCC; N = 4,054) using structural topic modeling. Methodologically, this approach offers one example of how recent advances in computational text analysis can augment traditional approaches to content analysis and serve to uncover theoretically important findings in large text corpora. By developing a method for ranking topics according to corpus coverage weighting (CCW), I also offer a new way to sift through large-k topic models.

This analysis provides evidence for the two countervailing trends modern clergy must contend with. On one hand, liberal pluralistic theologies developed since the 1960s have stripped pastoral language of its denominational specificity, leaving an ecumenical linguistic inclusivity THE CHANGING LANGUAGE OF PASTORAL CARE 363 that lets clients define the terms of their encounter. While arguably a welcome response to a hyperrationalized and impersonal medical system, this shift signals a ceding of professional authority as treatment is increasingly held on clients' grounds. On the other hand, recent calls for evidencebased practice have encouraged medical ministries to demonstrate efficacious and measurable treatment (Cadge 2012;James and Field 1992). Thus, today's pastoral care professionals must balance the bureaucratic push toward specificity with the simultaneous expectation of spiritual flexibility. Although this tension has always been a part of chaplaincy-as a religious profession situated in secular institutions-the recent increase in demographic and religious diversity only complicate the matter. Furthermore, while previous research has examined pastoral care from qualitative (Swift 2014) and historical (Holifield 2005) perspectives, there are no known studies that leverage the recent availability of large text corpora and contemporary analytic techniques to examine the linguistic dimension of declining religious authority among pastoral care professionals. This study addresses this important gap, offering insight into a seventy-year struggle to provide authentic religious care in a world of competing alternatives.

sections.header_raw: Declining Religious Authority in the 20th Century
sections.header_norm: declining religious authority in the 20th century
paragraphs: Debates about secularization have occupied sociologists since at least the classical theorist. Some have even argued that secularization gave rise to the discipline of sociology itself (Levine 1995;Lyman and Vidich 1987;Smith 2014). These debates continue apace today (cf. Schnabel and Bock 2017;Voas and Chaves 2018). Rather than weighing in on oft-cited measures of belief or religious attendance, I join Chaves ' (1994) attempt to move the secularization debate away from psychological beliefs and toward "the ways in which ideas and practices become mobilized and institutionalized in concrete social structures." (Chaves 1994:70). To this end, I examine the declining religious authority of pastoral care professionals as they navigate an increasingly crowded professional field. I draw heavily on Andrew Abbott's The System of Professions: An Essay on the Division of Expert Labor (1988), which develops a theory of jurisdictional conflict that takes place within an ecological field of expert authority. I briefly outline Abbott's theoretical framework before providing a short history of pastoral care in the 20th century.

paragraphs: In Abbott's (1988) framework, the system of professions resembles an ecological system wherein the public continually defines social problems while various interest groupsprofessions-compete to offer the best solution. Identifying the "best" solution is a messy process by which publics and professions alike consider the objective and subjective qualities of the problem at hand and assess the efficacy of a profession's solution. Ultimately, the winner of this competition is given jurisdictional authority to deal with the social problem. Alas, this victory is fleeting. Social conditions shift and new professions arise to challenge authority, offering new solutions to social problems. Thus, established professions must continually defend their professional jurisdiction. Within this ecological system, Abbott outlines four components of legitimate professions: diagnosis, inference, treatment, and academic knowledge.

First, a profession must be able to correctly diagnose relevant situations that fall within its professional jurisdiction. This screening process involves sorting through irrelevant information and classifying a given condition as a problem the profession can solve. Second, the profession must connect each diagnosis to a corresponding treatment using a system of professional inference. Here the profession must carefully balance public awareness: if the connection between diagnosis and treatment is too simple the profession is unnecessary, but if it is too complex, the public fails to grant their trust and authority. This often manifests in the professional balance of routine and nonroutine tasks: while routine tasks offer transparency but not prestige, nonroutine tasks offer prestige but not transparency.foot_0 Third, professional treatments must actually solve social problems. These solutions afford professions greater prestige to the extent that they are (1) exclusive, (2) specific, (3) measurably effective, and (4) speak to clients on its own terms. In other words, professions are most valued when they are the sole provider of specific treatments that work and communicate in professionally defined language. This last point is important to understand:

A profession clearly derives general social prestige from meeting clients on its own, rather than on their own, grounds, just as it derives prestige from strictly enforced rules of relevance. But a profession that forces clients to take treatment completely on its own terms risks heavy competition from those who talk to the clients in their own language…Only when a profession possesses absolute monopoly can it afford to ignore this arena of competition. (Abbott 1988:47) Thus, social prestige comes from having a professional monopoly that allows one to define the linguistic grounds of the exchange. Absent this monopoly, professions must court clients by speaking to them "on their own terms." Finally, Abbott (1988) describes academic knowledge as an abstract system that consolidates and codifies the three aforementioned processes within a profession. This system serves to present a logically consistent and rational system that justifies professional practices with respect to larger cultural values, while also working toward the improvement of diagnoses, treatments and inferential systems. Abbott's (1988) system serves as a helpful guide to understanding Chaves' (1994:767) conception of secularization as declining religious authority. Against the backdrop of structural differentiation and shifting occupational jurisdictions, one could analyze changing clerical diagnoses as they respond to encroaching professions: Do clergy remain an authority today on matters of family, divorce, addiction, health, and death? Or are they merely points of referral to occupations that have claimed this jurisdictional authority? Clerical treatments also emerge as the site of fruitful examination: to what extent are they exclusive, specific, measurably effective, and held on client grounds? Finally, one could consider the historical balance between clergy's routine and nonroutine tasks and attendant public impressions of prestige. The present article examines the academic knowledge system of pastoral care through a study of more than 4,000 peer-reviewed articles published over seven decades. Using pastoral language as a lens, I show how the diagnoses, inference, and treatment of personal problems have changed in response to the structural differentiation and social conditions of the last seventy years.

Before embarking on a historical analysis, some definitions are in order. Thus far I have used pastoral care professionals as a phrase that includes clergy, providers of pastoral care, chaplains, and pastoral counselors. I define clergy as any religious leader who is ordained or formally recognized by a religious community. Clergy responsibilities often include pastoral care, or "any form of personal ministry to individuals and to family and community relationships by representative religious persons (ordained and lay) and by their communities of faith, who understand and guide their caring efforts out of a theological perspective rooted in a tradition of faith," (Hunter 1990, quoted in Bunting 2000:385). When clergy routinely conduct pastoral care in secular institutions, they are referred to as chaplains. Although records of chaplaincy date back to at least the 1500s, its formalization in the 20th century, especially in the medical context after 1940, will emerge as an important part of the present narrative (Swift 2014). Finally, I take pastoral counseling to be "a more specialized and structured form of religiously based care, distinguished by its degree of contractual formality (a helping contract or covenant between the parties) and by the special expertise of the helping person," (Bunting 2000:387). While masking the imprecision with which these terms are generally used in the literature, these definitions should provide reasonable footing going forward.

sections.paper_id: Bernau - 2021 - From Christ to Compassion The Changing Language of Pastoral Care
paragraphs: Pastoral Care in America: 1900-1940 Until the early 20th century, clergy were more or less alone in their professional concern for "everyday problems" like marital strife, depression, and financial problems (Abbott 1988). Despite their many variations, when faced with these issues clerical diagnoses and treatments were generally predictable: the problem was sin and the solution was salvation. "Seasons of sorrow in families are opportunities which ought to be carefully improved by ministers. The providence of God is then preparing the sufferers for the cordial reception of the blessings of the Gospel" (Murphy 1877:249, quoted in Abbott 1988:282). Roman Catholic priests were some of the earliest adopters of a detailed diagnostic-treatment system. Upon receiving private confessions of the penitent, priests would assign one of many treatments: seven "Hail Marys," two "Our Fathers," etc. These treatments were exclusive and specific, but "success" was admittedly hard to measure. Importantly, clergy used explicitly religious language when talking with clients (Holifield 2005). Insofar as religious authorities held a monopoly on everyday problems, the clergy enjoyed an unchallenged jurisdiction.

Industrialization and attendant social changes at the turn of the 20th century introduced a host of new problems as people adjusted to new working conditions, city life, family changes, and the general loss of traditional ways of life (Douglas 1998). 2 The first jurisdictional challenge to the clergy's monopoly on personal problems came from neurology. Operating in the spirit of the ascendant medical system, neurologists searched for materialist cures for their catch-all diagnosis of personal problems as "nervousness." While hardly more measurable or "effective" than clerical treatments, early neurologists tapped into the cultural hope of medical rationality and built a robust system of academic knowledge. William James' Principles of Psychology was published in 1890, legitimating psychology as the new "science of human well-being" and by the 1920s, psychology had paired with neurology to produce both psychotherapy and psychiatrynew professional system that claimed to diagnose and treat a host of personal problems in a thoroughly medical context.

Of course, the clergy were interested in this new science of human well-being: "It was American pastoral theologians who first realized the potential of the new range of psychological and psychiatric disciplines that rose to prominence in Europe and America in the early twentieth century, and they sought to enter a constructive dialogue with these new disciplines" (Cornick 2000:374). One such example is the Emmanuel Movement, an early attempt to merge religion and psychotherapy that began in an Episcopal church in Boston around 1905. According to founders Elwood Worcester and Samuel McComb, every minister practiced psychotherapy whether they knew it or not, and the cure of souls should follow the latest medical science rather than received traditions (Powell 1909;Worcester and McComb 1909). Lyman Powell, an Episcopal priest and early proponent sums up the movement in the subtitle of his book: The Emmanuel Movement in a New England Town: A Systematic Account of Experiments and Reflections Designed to Determine the Proper Relationship Between the Minister and the Doctor in the Light of Modern 2 Voluntarist clubs sprouted in the early 1900s as a way to make up for the lost stability of agrarian community life in a modern urban landscape. The Knights of Columbus (est. 1882), Rotary Club (est. 1905), Kiwanis (est. 1914), and Lions (est. 1917) were all established in the early years of the 20th century.

Needs (Powell 1909). Both Worcester and McComb had received medical training in Europe and were thus able to straddle the jurisdictions of religion and medicine. The movement spread between 1905 and the 1920s, with participating churches holding open psychotherapeutic clinics, drawing hundreds of participants and receiving scores of letters (Macomber 1908;Worcester and McComb 1909). By the 1920s, the Emmanuel Movement lost its cultural traction on account of (1) a general confusion about the proper course and efficacy of treatment, and (2) the removal of support from the medical community. In 1930, Richard Cabot and Anton Boisen formed the Counsel for the Clinical Training of Theological Students, a similarly focused organization that institutionalized clinical pastoral education (CPE) in seminary curricula (Cornick 2000;Holifield 2005). This effort-then and now-advocates for the clergy's role in medical jurisdictions and better equips ministers to the changing scope of pastoral care. 3 Thus, early 20th-century clergy were faced with two countervailing trends. On one hand, the growth of neurology, psychology, psychotherapy, and psychiatry as autonomous disciplines signaled competing jurisdictional claims with regard to personal problems and issues of human well-being. On the other hand, a widespread interest in mental well-being brought renewed interest to their professional role. The rising tide of public interest seemed to lift all occupational boats. Nonetheless, religious authorities were forced to reconsider their profession in important ways.

It was a critical period for the Church because an inherited theological framework of sin, judgment and redemption leading to salvation was influenced by humanistic concerns which required a diminished theological base and a different goal, not now of salvation from sin but of personal responsibility and self-actualization or self-fulfillment. (Bunting 2000:387) Pastoral Care in America: 1940-1980

The largest sea-change in 20th-century pastoral care was the success of popular psychology after World War II. Although psychological conversations were bubbling in academic departments before the 1940s, post-War America saw a windfall of economic and social support for psychological endeavors. Spurred by a thriving economy, Congress passed the Mental Health Act in 1946, funding psychological research, training, and support for hundreds of colleges, seminars, and corporations (Biggart 1983). Carl Rogers, a humanistic psychotherapist who described his relationships with clients as one of "unconditional positive regard," found deep resonances with pastoral theologians (Kirschenbaum 1989;Rogers, 1942Rogers, , 1951)). In fact, after enrolling at Union Theological Seminary in the mid-1920s, Rogers took some of Harrison Elliot's earliest seminars in pastoral psychology (Holifield 2005:226). This client-centered approach championed self-realization over previous ideas about moral adjustment. Social institutions were often seen as constraining efforts to build and express the "real self." This inward turn has a long history in the march of Western individualism (Taylor 1992), and Rogers' work set the academic foundation for the larger cultural movement of the 1960s. With the popularity of psychotherapy, the success of CPE, and the cultural values of post-War America, pastoral care professionals began to replace pastoral care with pastoral counseling when describing their profession. "Care" signaled a broad collection of ministerial duties, whereas "counseling" borrowed prestige from the medical professions and signaled an engagement with the psychotherapeutic academic knowledge system (Bunting 2000;Rieff 1966).

3 Despite their early agreement, Cabot and Boisen's theological differences led to a rift between Cabot's "Boston Tradition" and Boisen's "New York Tradition." While Cabot emphasized a brand of moral legalism and spiritual formation, Boisen-influenced in part by the work of George Herbert Mead-saw mental illness as a failure to live up to internalized social standards and promoted a moral freedom from societal pressure (Holifield 2005:235-49).

Pastoral counselors began to speak a new language after the Second World War. Gone from pastoral conversations were the labored explanations and the bits and pieces of well-meant advice. Gone was the tendency to view counseling as a theological debate or moral exhortation. And gone also was the assumption that pastors were supposed to inform people that they suffered from compulsiveness or an inferiority complex. (Holifield 2005:259) The attenuation of theological dogma and the adoption of positive psychotherapeutic language opened the door for more chaplains to find permanent positions in secular institutions like prisons, schools, and hospitals in the 1950s and 1960s (Ford and Tartaglia 2006;Swift 2014).

In 1960s America, religious institutions experienced the largest seismic shock amid the social changes of this tumultuous decade. Young adults' weekly service attendance nearly halved-from 51 percent in the 1950s to 28 percent in 1960s-and older generations increasingly sought out nontraditional forms of spirituality (Bellah et al. 1985;Putnam and Campbell 2012;Tipton 1982). This religious pluralism was met by a new wave of interfaith theologies seeking to smooth the edges of an escalating religious culture war. Among these theologians was Karl Rahner, a Catholic thinker who wrote extensively about the changing church of the 1960s and 1970s. His theology interpreted the inherent grace of human nature as evidence of salvation, irrespective of Christian revelation. In this way, all humans are "anonymous Christians" and should be welcomed by the Catholic church as such (Bunting 2000). Rahner's liberal interfaith theology went on to influence the pivotal meeting at the Second Vatican Council of 1962, wherein the church addressed its relationship to the modern world. John Hick, a prominent philosopher of religion, proposed a similar theological system wherein all world religions are manifestations of "the Real"-a universally valid notion of transcendent reality (Knitter 2002). In this way, Hick's philosophy saw each religious tradition on equal footing, offering something new to our understanding of religious experience.

While these systems spoke to the pluralizing religious context of the 1960s, they came under attack during a conservative religious backlash of the 1970s and 1980s (Putnam and Campbell 2012). "The strong individualistic understanding of personal growth, together with the non-directive and unconditionally accepting method of pastoral counseling was coming under criticism," (Bunting 2000:389). Many complained that psychology had edged out theology to make pastoral care a mere shadow of the social sciences. "Some now believe the pastoral counseling movement has not always been able adequately to integrate the theology and practice of pastoral care so as to do justice both to Christian doctrine and to a psychological understanding of human beings" (Bunting 2000:388, emphasis added). Conservative theologians like Thomas Oden, whose brand of "paleo-orthodoxy" spoke to a return to moral principles, led a movement to recover the historical wisdom of theology as it applied to pastoral care situations (Browning 1983;Bunting 2000). Echoing the 19th-century conception of moral formation, they argued that religious authorities are often called on to give explicit moral direction; the mere existence of multiple options does not preclude the necessity of a moral evaluation. Although this backlash against theological pluralism created waves in some circles, it is hard to say whether the American people followed in this direction. Wuthnow (1990) argues that by the 1980s this split between liberal and conservative theology had effectively "restructured" American religion, with religious conservatives on one side and religious liberals on the other. On account of interregional migration and declining educational differences, the once-stark denominational divides were loosened, allowing new issue-based coalitions to form. According to Putnam and Campbell (2012) and Chaves (2017), however, the evangelical revival observed in the 1980s was not a result of more conservative theology. Biblical literalism has been steadily declining since the 1950s and Americans are increasingly accepting of religious "others." In fact, seventy percent of Americans agree that religions other than their own can lead to eternal life (Chaves 2017), and half of all marriages today occur across religious traditions (Putnam and Campbell 2012).

sections.paper_id: Bernau - 2021 - From Christ to Compassion The Changing Language of Pastoral Care
paragraphs: Although it is tempting to emphasize the similarities of disparate religions in the name of cooperation and acceptance, many religious authorities argue that the next step for religious leaders is a charitable but strong emphasis on religion differences (Prothero 2011). Rather than subsuming all beliefs under a vague notion like Rahner's "universal Christianity" or Hick's "the Real," modern seminary courses are increasingly set on exposing new clergy and chaplains to world religions and faiths that depart from their own (Clooney 2010;Knitter 2013). As individuals find it increasingly difficult to identify fully with one tradition, clergy encourage "multiple religious belongings," leading individuals to find unique ways of supplementing their faith with concepts and ideas garnered from their religious neighbors (Cornille 2010;Knitter 2013;Largen 2013;Taylor 2020).

This professional emphasis on postmodern pluralism is enacted in the way chaplains talk about their occupation. Contrary to the 17th-century "cartographers of the soul" pointing the way to salvation, today's pastoral care manuals stress the role of the chaplain as a journeying partner and spiritual companion. Modern chaplains must improvise when the metaphorical "map" of another's religious faith is placed in front of them. This fluidity is also displayed when chaplains let the patient define terms that are meaningful to them (Cadge 2012). When a patient refers to "respect" or "hope" the chaplain is encouraged to help unpack these concepts: "Such conversations allowed rich descriptions of these words, making them more meaningful to [the patient] and more relevant in helping to address spiritual needs." (Fitchett and Nolan 2015:220). In a social context increasingly populated by individuals without an explicit religious tradition, chaplains help people define the terms of their spirituality. Garces-Foley (2013) speaks to the politics behind the changing language of "spirituality," arguing that this ostensible shift toward openness in fact serves to alienate those seeking religious consolation.

In short, the latter half of the 20th century introduced social changes that pastoral care professionals are still grappling with today. Psychologists, psychotherapist, and other secular professionals were spared the imperative to construct and address interfaith theological positions, freeing time and energy to advance and consolidate respective academic knowledge systems. As religious attendance has declined, seminarians today are increasingly finding work outside of traditional parish life where they must navigate a unique social space of multiple religious belonging (Cadge, Freese, and Christakis 2008). Careful to avoid the "residual antagonism to the Christian Church left over from the experience of colonialism" (Lamb 2000:454), chaplains let patients introduce their own spiritual beliefs without prompting, and follow these metaphors wherever they may lead. Theological doctrines have offered ways of squaring this practice with the Christian tradition, to varying degrees of acceptance. Although postmodern pluralism offers an easy solution to stubborn religious differences, there is an increasing emphasis on the value and recognition of these differences. Seminaries have attempted to address this issue, although the importance of interfaith dialogue as part of a formalized curriculum is still in its early stages.

Modern chaplains must improvise when the metaphorical "map" of another's religious faith is placed in front of them. This fluidity is also displayed when chaplains let the patient define terms that are meaningful to them (Cadge 2012). When a patient refers to "respect" or "hope" the chaplain is encouraged to help unpack these concepts: "Such conversations allowed rich descriptions of these words, making them more meaningful to [the patient] and more relevant in helping to address spiritual needs." (Fitchett and Nolan 2015:220). In a social context increasingly populated by individuals without an explicit religious tradition, chaplains help people define the terms of their spirituality. Garces-Foley (2013) speaks to the politics behind the changing language of "spirituality," arguing that this ostensible shift toward openness in fact serves to alienate those seeking religious consolation.

In short, the latter half of the 20th century introduced social changes that pastoral care professionals are still grappling with today. Psychologists, psychotherapist, and other secular professionals were spared the imperative to construct and address interfaith theological positions, freeing time and energy to advance and consolidate respective academic knowledge systems. As religious attendance has declined, seminarians today are increasingly finding work outside of traditional parish life where they must navigate a unique social space of multiple religious belonging (Cadge, Freese, and Christakis 2008). Careful to avoid the "residual antagonism to the Christian Church left over from the experience of colonialism" (Lamb 2000:454), chaplains let patients introduce their own spiritual beliefs without prompting, and follow these metaphors wherever they may lead. Theological doctrines have offered ways of squaring this practice with the Christian tradition, to varying degrees of acceptance. Although postmodern pluralism offers an easy solution to stubborn religious differences, there is an increasing emphasis on the value and recognition of these differences. Seminaries have attempted to address this issue, although the importance of interfaith dialogue as part of a formalized curriculum is still in its early stages.

sections.paper_id: Bernau - 2021 - From Christ to Compassion The Changing Language of Pastoral Care
sections.header_raw: Pastoral Care as a Professional Occupation
sections.header_norm: pastoral care as a professional occupation
paragraphs: With this historical context, it is instructive to view the last century of pastoral care through Abbott's theory of professional systems and jurisdictional authority. The expansion of the medicalized fields like psychotherapy and psychiatry, as well as nonmedical occupations like marriage counselors and divorce lawyers, have annexed professional ground once the exclusive reign of religious authorities before the 20th century. Certainly, individuals may consult religious authorities for advice, but upon diagnosis and classification of the problem, pastoral care professionals today simply have more referral options to secular occupations that claim expert authority on a range of personal problems. This jurisdictional secession often causes confusion over professional responsibilities, especially for chaplains who work in secular institutions. Many doctors and staff members think chaplains simply perform religious rituals and prayers, while chaplains characterize their work in broader terms: giving a sense of wholeness, healing, and presence to the patient (Cadge, Calle, and Dillinger 2011). These divergent expectations are a significant source of stress, especially among chaplains working in hospice (Williams et al. 2004). In an effort to alleviate these identity concerns, a group of scholars developed a "taxonomy" of 100 chaplain activities aimed to improve communication and coordination among palliative care teams (Massey et al. 2015). In a similar study, Idler et al. (2015) classify the frequent activities of healthcare chaplains into two groups: "doing" and "being." Although existential activities like active listening and spiritual assessment fall under "being," many chaplains also find themselves in a more active or "doing" role: filling out advance directives with the patient, providing food, etc. Examining 1,140 recorded chaplain visits, Idler et al. (2015) found most involved some "doing" activity. Contrary to what many doctors and staff members think, chaplains do surprisingly few overtly religious practices (i.e., administering sacraments, reading scripture): only three percent of recorded visits involved these activities. In addition to chaplain activities, Idler et al. (2015) also analyzed more than 1,500 conversations between chaplains, patients, and their families. Analogous to the activity clusters, conversations fell into two groups: "practical matters" and "ultimate concerns." Although ultimate concerns (i.e., emotions, religious and existential matters) were discussed in more than half of the conversations, practical matters (i.e., financial concerns, hospice care, advance directives) were discussed more frequently: 75 percent of patient conversations and 84 percent of family conversations. Whether this practical emphasis is indicative of patient changes (diminished religiosity) or the chaplaincy profession (passive roles, liberal theology, etc.) is hard to say. 4Turning to Abbott's criteria of socially legitimate professions, the treatment offered by today's pastoral care professionals puts the profession in a difficult position. First, the spiritual "effectiveness" of pastoral care has never been easy to measure, and the recent push for evidencebased practice only speaks to the increasing need and urgency of this justification (Cadge 2012). Second, the occupation presents few routine tasks capable of outsourcing to subordinates (although recent attempts to "automate" religious counsel are not unheard of; see Heilweil 2019). Third, specificity and exclusivity in pastoral care treatments have been jettisoned as a result of liberal pluralist theologies and secular alternatives. Each of these characteristics, some inherent to the profession and some recent developments, diminish the social prestige and authority of pastoral care professionals. Importantly, the role of language becomes one of primary importance here. Clergy in the past could afford to speak in explicitly religious language on account of their unchallenged monopoly on personal problems, whereas today's pastoral care professionals make it a point of emphasis to "meet people where they are" and let them define the terms and direction of their time together. Faced with increasing competition and declining authority, pastoral care professionals' ecumenical language can be seen as a necessary attempt to regain client favor in a crowded system of professions.

Of course, this is not to make a normative statement against recent pastoral developments. In a hyper-rationalized medical institution, for example, nurses and physicians may overemphasize the diagnostic process, stripping away too much personal information as irrelevant and leaving the patient feeling identity-less (Kübler-Ross 1969;Starr 1982). In this way, the hospital chaplain provides a valuable service in recognizing and affirming patients in a holistic, unconditional way. Nonetheless, social prestige fails to come to those without measurable treatments or professional language. Data from the General Social Survey show those with "a great deal of confidence" in leaders of religious organizations fell from 45 to 30 percent among regular church attendees between 1972 and 2014; among all adults, these numbers fell from 35 to 20 percent (Chaves 2017).

Importantly, as a response to diminishing jurisdictions professions may respond with new roles and divisions of labor. The rise of healthcare chaplaincy is in large part a response to these shifting jurisdictional waters. Although there have been very few national studies on the prominence of healthcare chaplaincy (see Cadge et al. 2008 for an exception), religious adherence has decreased and hospital sizes have increased since at least the 1960s in America (Kaufman 2006;Starr 1982;Voas and Chaves 2016). This has shifted the visibility of religious professions away from traditional congregational roles and toward medical roles like that of a hospital chaplain. Cadge (2012) and Swift (2014) both document the emergence of this set-apart ministerial profession, including its professionalization efforts at the turn of the century (VandeCreek and Burton 2001), and Abbott (1988) emphasizes the persistent role of the chaplain in modern medical institutions. Previous research estimates the presence of chaplains in half to two-thirds of all U.S. hospitals, and most chaplains visit well over half of all admitted patients (Cadge 2012;Cadge et al. 2008). As Idler et al. (2015) argue, this presents somewhat of an anomaly: at once seated in a profession rapidly seceding jurisdictional authority, medical chaplaincy remains a ubiquitous presence in modern life. How do these professionals navigate their secular yet spiritual occupation? How has the language of pastoral care shifted in response to this recent emphasis?

sections.paper_id: Bernau - 2021 - From Christ to Compassion The Changing Language of Pastoral Care
paragraphs: In this study, I use recent techniques for computational text analysis to examine the academic knowledge system of pastoral care professionals and its recent evolution. This knowledge system serves to present a logically consistent and rational structure that justifies professional practices with respect to larger cultural values, and the recent availability of large-scale digital text data facilitates an examination of the sweeping historical trends described above. To examine the changing nature of religious authority in this context, I examine 71 years of academic articles from the JPCC. This journal was one of the first publications to emerge from the fledgling pastoral care movement of the post-War years. With seven decades of continuous publication, it remains the longest-running journal in its field and boasts a diverse coalition of member organizations, including the Canadian Association of Spiritual Care (CASC), the National Association of Catholic Chaplains (NACC), and Neshama: Association of Jewish Chaplains (NAJC). Table 1 provides more detail on these organizations and their association with the JPCC. 5Compared to similar journals, the JPCC has a deliberately interdisciplinary focus: "to advance theory and professional practice through scholarly and reflective literature on pastoral and spiritual care, counseling, psychotherapy, education, and research" (JPCC Inc. 2019). This wide substantive net and seven-decade history makes this journal an ideal vantage point from which to observe conflicts of jurisdictional authority. I collected the full text for each of the substantive articles published between 1947 and 2018 (N = 4,054) through institutional subscriptions managed by EBSCO Host and SAGE journals. 6 The resulting data set contained full text and metadata for all 4,054 substantive articles published by the JPCC between 1947 and 2018 (see Figure 1). Note: N = 4,054

I used structural topic modeling to identify longitudinal themes in this corpus of academic articles. Developed in 2013, structural topic modeling (STM) builds on Blei and Lafferty's (2006) early work on latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) topic modeling and its dynamic variants (Blei 2012;Roberts, Stewart, and Tingley 2013). Topic modeling has received much attention in cultural sociology for its ability to inductively "code" a large text corpus for emergent themes (Bail 2014;DiMaggio 2015;DiMaggio, Nag, and Blei 2013;Kinney, Davis, and Zhang 2018). The main contribution of structural topic modeling over traditional LDA is the ability to account for document-level metadata variables to facilitate better estimations of topics (Roberts et al., 2013(Roberts et al., , 2014)). Specifically, STM facilitates the use of metadata to estimate either topic prevalence or topic 2019) to extract metadata from file names generated by Zotero. PDF copies were deleted before proceeding with the next year of data collection. content. When used to estimate topic prevalence, STM uses document variables to determine predicted proportions of a topic in a given document (gamma matrix). When used to estimate topic content, STM uses document variables to determine the topic-word compositions (beta matrix). In the present analysis, I employ this functionality to allow topic prevalence to vary by year. Paired with 4,054 full-text articles, this technique allows an efficient and comprehensive method to examine how pastoral care professionals discussion exemplified fluctuating claims of jurisdictional authority in the latter half of the 20th century.

After preprocessing the text data, the final corpus consisted of over 2.5 million word tokens. My analytical approach proceeded in three stages. First, I used Lee and Mimno's (2014) algorithm to identify an approximate number of topics that solves for the convex hull of the word co-occurrence matrix. After five runs, this algorithm returned an average of 74 topics. Second, I compared a range of related models based on Roberts et al.'s (2013) "goodness of fit" measurements. These measures include held-out likelihood (Wallach et al. 2009), residual analysis (Taddy 2012), semantic coherence (Mimno et al. 2011), and topic exclusivity (Airoldi and Bischof 2016). After testing models ranging from 20 to 100 topics, 70 to 80 emerged as a desirable topic range, confirming the results of Lee and Mimno's (2014) algorithm. Third, I tested each model with 70-80 topics (70, 71, 72…80), ultimately arriving at 74 as the number of topics that satisfied this suite of fit statistics. Model in hand, a human reader still finds it hard to interpret a 74-topic model. Presented with endless word probabilities and document compositions, some have likened the researcher's task to "reading the tea leaves" (Chang et al. 2009). To balance the need for a model with sufficient complexity without sacrificing substantive interpretability, I developed a method for weighting topics according to their corpus coverage. I calculate this CCW by using the returned gamma matrix (document-topic proportions) to create a count of how many articles fall under different document proportion thresholds (10-60 percent) for each topic. I then weight these counts according to the document proportion threshold. Formally,

where for each topic K, N x represents the number of documents that contain at least x percent of topic K. This CCW balances topics that have both breadth (covering a small amount of many documents) with depth (covering a large amount of few documents) to aid interpretation of large topic models. Once weighted, I subset the top twenty topics (about one-third of the model) for subsequent analysis and select ten for presentation below. I also supplement this data-driven topic selection with an additional ten topics chosen for substantive interpretation.

To balance the need for a model with sufficient complexity without sacrificing substantive interpretability, I developed a method for weighting topics according to their corpus coverage. I calculate this CCW by using the returned gamma matrix (document-topic proportions) to create a count of how many articles fall under different document proportion thresholds (10-60 percent) for each topic. In this study, I use recent techniques for computational text analysis to examine the academic knowledge system of pastoral care professionals and its recent evolution. This knowledge system serves to present a logically consistent and rational structure that justifies professional practices with respect to larger cultural values, and the recent availability of large-scale digital text data facilitates an examination of the sweeping historical trends described above. To examine the changing nature of religious authority in this context, I examine 71 years of academic articles from the JPCC. This journal was one of the first publications to emerge from the fledgling pastoral care movement of the post-War years. With seven decades of continuous publication, it remains the longest-running journal in its field and boasts a diverse coalition of member organizations, including the Canadian Association of Spiritual Care (CASC), the National Association of Catholic Chaplains (NACC), and Neshama: Association of Jewish Chaplains (NAJC). Table 1 provides more detail on these organizations and their association with the JPCC. 5Compared to similar journals, the JPCC has a deliberately interdisciplinary focus: "to advance theory and professional practice through scholarly and reflective literature on pastoral and spiritual care, counseling, psychotherapy, education, and research" (JPCC Inc. 2019). This wide substantive net and seven-decade history makes this journal an ideal vantage point from which to observe conflicts of jurisdictional authority. I collected the full text for each of the substantive articles published between 1947 and 2018 (N = 4,054) through institutional subscriptions managed by EBSCO Host and SAGE journals. 6 The resulting data set contained full text and metadata for all 4,054 substantive articles published by the JPCC between 1947 and 2018 (see Figure 1). The main contribution of structural topic modeling over traditional LDA is the ability to account for document-level metadata variables to facilitate better estimations of topics (Roberts et al., 2013(Roberts et al., , 2014)). Specifically, STM facilitates the use of metadata to estimate either topic prevalence or topic 2019) to extract metadata from file names generated by Zotero. When used to estimate topic prevalence, STM uses document variables to determine predicted proportions of a topic in a given document (gamma matrix). sections.header_raw: Descendant Themes: Human Nature/Traditional Religious Language
sections.header_norm: descendant themes human nature traditional religious language
paragraphs: Topic 45 in Figure 2 suggests a decline in broad language about our fundamental human or moral nature. I argue that this sharp decline in topic 45 signals a turn to individualistic language in recent decades. The third topic in Figure 2 represents an unambiguous decline in traditional religious language, especially between 1947 and 1970. The top five words in this topic-God, Christian, Jesus, Christ, and church-signal strong semantic exclusivity. At the beginning of the 1950s, this topic comprised about five percent of the content in all articles, dropping to 2.5 percent by 1970 and hovering around one percent today. This trend mirrors larger trends in American religiosity as documented by Chaves (2017), Putnam and Campbell (2012), Wuthnow (1990), and Bellah et al. (1985) and is particularly striking within the pages of a professional journal on pastoral care.

paragraphs: These descendant themes are paired with ascendant themes of love (topic 43), patient (topic 61), and phenomenology (topic 10) presented in Figure 3. The inclusion of god in the top-ten words that make up topic 43 suggests a changing language of divinity in today's pastoral care. God was found in the company of words like Jesus, Christ, Christian, and church in the 1950s, whereas today it is accompanied by a suite of ecumenical terms: love, heart, breath, hand, life, care, tree, mother, sing, and music. The centrality of patient in topic 61 signals the increasingly medical context in which pastoral care occurs today, surrounded by attendant words like chaplain, hospital, staff, visit, and nurse. Finally, topic 10 in Figure 3 demonstrates modern clergy's increasingly phenomenological approach to their professional duties. Patient's stories, experience, and narrative construction are welcomed by an occupation whose professional identity is increasingly focused on listening, reflection, and conversation.

paragraphs: Figures 4 and 5 present an additional ten topics that, while not falling in the top third of topics by CCW, nonetheless represent important substantive trends illuminated by the present analysis. The first two topics in Figure 4 represent the aforementioned theological debates in post-1960 America. Responding to rising religious pluralism and counter-culture movements, the theology of the pastoral profession came under scrutiny. As Abbott (1988) notes, when the divergence between practice and abstract theory becomes too great, internal professional conflict ensues. Of particular note is the surfacing of Anton Boisen and Richard Cabot by name in topic 71. As the founders of the modern CPE movement, their inclusion in this topic offers some face validity to the thematic topics identified in this analysis. The next two topics in Figure 4 demonstrate the early emphasis on psychoanalysis-especially the work of Sigmund Freud and Oskar Pfister in topic 1-and the mental health movement of post-WWII America in topic 14. I argue that the declining prevalence of these topics suggest a resolved jurisdictional dispute that arose at the turn of the century (and partly inspired the JPCC's formation). As subsequent decades brought professional developments in both fields, these competing occupations were institutionalized and developed new jurisdictional lines, diminishing the need for cross-disciplinary language in recent decades. The last topic in Figure 4 (topic 56) similarly reflects professional development as new techniques for efficacy and measurement fill recent academic articles on pastoral care. This trend speaks to today's calls for evidence-based chaplaincy and the historical difficulty in measuring pastoral care treatments. Care andCounseling, 1947-2018 Turning to Figure 5, topic 36 demonstrates the declining discussion of marriage and divorce as these issues increasingly fall to other professional jurisdictions. Topic 48 suggests an institutionalization of Carl Rogers' "client-centered therapy" as pastoral care professionals increasingly deal with issues of forgiveness, relationships, shame, and empathy. Although, this finding should be considered in step with topic 20 in Figure 2: rather than a general decline in emotionality, these two topics may suggest a change in emotional emphasis from individuals to relationships. Topic 30 shows the precipitous increase of pastoral discussions about post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)-perhaps due to increased foreign conflict after the September 11th terrorist attacks. Straddling the line between mental health and cultural trauma, PTSD may occupy a difficult place in the professional system and religious authorities could be uniquely placed to deal with this population. Finally, topics 54 and 55 provide evidence for the increasing concern surrounding death and dying for today's pastoral care professionals. Research on contemporary attitudes toward death and dying speaks to the increased anxiety afforded by a recent cultural developments, from a hyper-rationalized medical system to declining religious authority (Bendle 2001;Mellor 1992).

The shift from universal to particular language, and from religious to spiritual language may also signal new methods of inter-professional division of labor. As healthcare chaplaincy gains visibility over traditional congregational leadership, this linguistic shift signals new expectations for a new pastoral profession (Cadge 2012). Figure 6 presents raw word counts of four related terms: chaplain, clergy, pastor, and priest. As made apparent, discussions of chaplains have dominated the focus of pastoral care since the 1980s. This emerges as an important reaction to declining religious authority. To gain legitimacy in this secular space, chaplaincy had to eschew overt religious language in favor of modern individualistic spiritual conversations.

sections.paper_id: Bernau - 2021 - From Christ to Compassion The Changing Language of Pastoral Care
paragraphs: This article documents the changing language of pastoral care in the 20th century. I pair Chaves' (1994) theory of declining religious authority with Abbott's (1988) theory of professional systems to document the competing jurisdictional struggle of 20th-century pastoral care. I do this by combining a historical analysis of related professions with a quantitative analysis of the evolution of pastoral language over more than seventy years, as evidenced through academic articles in the JPCC (N = 4,054). I use structural topic modeling to estimate a 74-topic model using year of publication as a topic prevalence covariate. Although impossible to summarize seven decades of academic writing, three important conclusions can be taken from the results presented here.

First, between 1947 and 2018 the language of pastoral care eschewed sweeping discussions of human nature and mankind for an individualistic phenomenology of personal narrative and experience (cf. Figure 2 and 3; topic 45 vs. topic 10). As Holifield (2005) documents, this concern with "nature" was a hallmark of 19th-century pastoral theology as clergy tried to assess the appropriate balance between the "law" of effort and the "gospel" of relaxation. Today, heightened attention to religious pluralism and cultural diversity has narrowed pastoral discussions to focus on individual experience and the construction of personal meaning. This finding reflects previous sociological attention to American religious individualism, as elaborated by Bellah (1970), Bloom (1992), and others (Beyerlein and Vaisey 2013;Hewitt 1989;Madsen 2009;Rieff 1966).

The second important conclusion is the changing language of religiosity since 1947. In the 1950s, nearly 5 percent of pastoral care articles dealt with traditional Christian terminology: Jesus, Christ, God, Christian, church, etc. Today, this percentage hovers near one percent (see Figure 2; topic 31). In its place there appears a new language of quasi-secular spirituality: love, heart, breath, god, tree, mother, sing, etc. (cf. Figure 3; topic 43). I argue this linguistic shift represents a direct response to social changes in the latter half of the 20th century. As clergy face declining religious belief, behavior, and belonging, as well as rising immigration and multiple religious belonging (Chaves 2017;Cornille 2010), their ministry has responded by scrubbing their professional language of denominational specificity and opening the door to more inviting and ecumenical language. This finding strongly reflects Wuthnow's (1990) account of the decline of denominationalism in the latter 20th century. Furthermore, while this may offer short-term professional advantage over linguistically exclusive professions, it also threatens perceptions of authority, expertise, and prestige. My analysis supports this idea by documenting the dynamic transition from pastoral linguistic exclusivity in the 1950s to an ecumenical linguistic inclusivity in the 21st century.

Finally, this research documents the diminishing discussions about the related professions of psychology, psychoanalysts, and psychiatry among pastoral care professionals between 1947 and 2018. While contrary to initial expectations, this decline may signal a solidification of jurisdictional boundaries among multiple related professions that emerged in the mid-20th century. When the JPCC began, there was a lively debate about the relationship between psychotherapy and religious professionals. Today, these concerns appear to have abated (see Figure 4; topics 1 and 14). However, my results show a heightened emphasis on issues of research and measurement, as chaplains confront recent calls for evidence-based practice (Cadge et al. 2011).

Together these findings shed light on the declining religious authority of pastoral care professionals in the 20th century. As clergy lost their professional monopoly in the 20th century, they have responded by replacing overtly religious terminology with individualistic language, and employ liberal pluralistic theologies while attempting to balance the push for specificity and evidence-based treatments found within secular institutions. The contemporary prominence of chaplaincy is an important reaction to declining religious authority, as the ministerial profession increases efforts within secular institutions (Cadge et al. 2008). The findings presented here reveal a heightened concentration on chaplaincy after the 1980s and document the important linguistic differences that result from this new division of labor.
sections.text:
This article documents the changing language of pastoral care in the 20th century. I pair Chaves' (1994) theory of declining religious authority with Abbott's (1988) theory of professional systems to document the competing jurisdictional struggle of 20th-century pastoral care. I do this by combining a historical analysis of related professions with a quantitative analysis of the evolution of pastoral language over more than seventy years, as evidenced through academic articles in the JPCC (N = 4,054). I use structural topic modeling to estimate a 74-topic model using year of publication as a topic prevalence covariate. Although impossible to summarize seven decades of academic writing, three important conclusions can be taken from the results presented here.

First, between 1947 and 2018 the language of pastoral care eschewed sweeping discussions of human nature and mankind for an individualistic phenomenology of personal narrative and experience (cf. Figure 2 and 3; topic 45 vs. topic 10). As Holifield (2005) documents, this concern with "nature" was a hallmark of 19th-century pastoral theology as clergy tried to assess the appropriate balance between the "law" of effort and the "gospel" of relaxation. Today, heightened attention to religious pluralism and cultural diversity has narrowed pastoral discussions to focus on individual experience and the construction of personal meaning. This finding reflects previous sociological attention to American religious individualism, as elaborated by Bellah (1970), Bloom (1992), and others (Beyerlein and Vaisey 2013;Hewitt 1989;Madsen 2009;Rieff 1966).

The second important conclusion is the changing language of religiosity since 1947. In the 1950s, nearly 5 percent of pastoral care articles dealt with traditional Christian terminology: Jesus, Christ, God, Christian, church, etc. Today, this percentage hovers near one percent (see Figure 2; topic 31). In its place there appears a new language of quasi-secular spirituality: love, heart, breath, god, tree, mother, sing, etc. (cf. Figure 3; topic 43). I argue this linguistic shift represents a direct response to social changes in the latter half of the 20th century. As clergy face declining religious belief, behavior, and belonging, as well as rising immigration and multiple religious belonging (Chaves 2017;Cornille 2010), their ministry has responded by scrubbing their professional language of denominational specificity and opening the door to more inviting and ecumenical language. This finding strongly reflects Wuthnow's (1990) account of the decline of denominationalism in the latter 20th century. Furthermore, while this may offer short-term professional advantage over linguistically exclusive professions, it also threatens perceptions of authority, expertise, and prestige. My analysis supports this idea by documenting the dynamic transition from pastoral linguistic exclusivity in the 1950s to an ecumenical linguistic inclusivity in the 21st century.

Finally, this research documents the diminishing discussions about the related professions of psychology, psychoanalysts, and psychiatry among pastoral care professionals between 1947 and 2018. While contrary to initial expectations, this decline may signal a solidification of jurisdictional boundaries among multiple related professions that emerged in the mid-20th century. When the JPCC began, there was a lively debate about the relationship between psychotherapy and religious professionals. Today, these concerns appear to have abated (see Figure 4; topics 1 and 14). However, my results show a heightened emphasis on issues of research and measurement, as chaplains confront recent calls for evidence-based practice (Cadge et al. 2011).

Together these findings shed light on the declining religious authority of pastoral care professionals in the 20th century. As clergy lost their professional monopoly in the 20th century, they have responded by replacing overtly religious terminology with individualistic language, and employ liberal pluralistic theologies while attempting to balance the push for specificity and evidence-based treatments found within secular institutions. The contemporary prominence of chaplaincy is an important reaction to declining religious authority, as the ministerial profession increases efforts within secular institutions (Cadge et al. 2008). The findings presented here reveal a heightened concentration on chaplaincy after the 1980s and document the important linguistic differences that result from this new division of labor.

sections.paper_id: Bernau - 2021 - From Christ to Compassion The Changing Language of Pastoral Care
paragraphs: Although this study provides a model for how computational text analysis can inform both historical and theoretical debates, subsequent research should apply this model to other types of pastoral and religious texts. Although the JPCC remains one of the longest running journals in the field, future work should explore journals that might occupy other places on the theological spectrum. How have more liberal or more conservative publications responded linguistically to the social changes expounded above? In his work on American Evangelicals, Smith (1998) describes a kind of cultural doubling-down as religious minorities thrive on the construction of antagonistic external pressures. To the extent that this identity work is linguistic, longitudinal text analysis in this community of discourse would provide important evidence for this social-psychological behavior.

Future work should employ this model to test other aspects of Abbott's professional system. Although the present study focused on the academic knowledge system, the availability of diagnostic manuals and treatment reports present fruitful data sources for longitudinal linguistic analysis. In particular, a focus on the legal dimension of jurisdictional claims would showcase another side of pastoral authority. A legally enforced monopoly on professional practice remains the ultimate social legitimation, and in her book A Ministry of Presence: Chaplaincy, Spiritual Care, and the Law, Sullivan (2014) documents the liminal legal space that modern chaplains occupy: at once a religious professional yet employed by a secular institution to care for an ill-defined and always-changing clientele. Subsequent research should explore the changing legal landscape of personal problems in response to the historical changes elaborated above.

While structural topic modeling allows a high-level view of linguistic shifts, future research should pair this technique with traditional content analysis and historical close readings. As DiMaggio (2013) and Marshall (2013) attest, the results of topic modeling are dependent on the researcher's subject expertise and those capitalizing on the recent boon of digital text should be wary not to discount the rich and informative historical scholarship on these issues. Although the present study relied heavily on Holifield's (2005) historical study of pastoral care, future research in this vein might focus on Laderman's (1999Laderman's ( , 2005) ) historical study of death in America, Collins' (1998) study of philosophical evolution, or Szacki's (1979) history of sociological thought. The availability of large-scale digital text repositories only makes this type of research more feasible (Bernau 2018).

Finally, this study speaks to larger theoretical concerns in sociology that could benefit from recent methodological advances in text analysis. In particular, the rise of individualized pastoral care language is aligned with Meyer's (2010) institutional analysis of individual centrality in modern society. Future research might look to other professional spheres to probe the degree of "individualization" present across multiple disciplines. Likewise, the emphasis on personal experience, narrative, and phenomenology in today's pastoral care journals is congruent with Rieff's (1966) "triumph of the therapeutic" and the changing role of religion in modern society. Future research might look at generalist religious dialogue rather than pastoral care in an attempt to see how far this therapeutic trend has diffused. The results of this study, along with these future directions, demonstrate how the rise of computational text analysis and attendant availability of digital text opens the door to historical sociologists looking to probe the linguistic landscape of the social world.
